originalTitle: Morning Mail: Queensland buries DV report, rate hike fears grow, Zendaya’s cultural ‘hijack’
summary: Our exclusive story reveals how the state government sidelined a report critical of police; US-Iran flare-up threatens RBA hike
content: Morning everyone. The Liberal National government in Queensland has repeatedly said it would “put victims at the heart” of the state’s justice policies – but we have an exclusive report today on how a panel of victim-survivors of domestic violence saw their report buried after it slammed police handling of cases and called for civilian reviews. Plus: the incoming Labor president says concerns about personal safety are hindering how politicians do their job, economists say the renewal of the Middle East conflict makes rate hikes more likely here, and Zendaya has been accused of lacking “cultural knowledge” by wearing ancient jewellery. Australia ‘Make a difference’ | The incoming Labor president, Kate Ellis, says toxicity in politics is spilling into the broader community and public debate, warning threats against MPs’ safety are limiting how elected politicians do their jobs. Exclusive | The Queensland government has buried the findings of its own working group of domestic violence victim-survivors, after their report was heavily critical of police responses and called for a “civilian review mechanism” to escalate complaints about police. Surgeon banned | A surgeon has been found guilty of professional misconduct and barred from practising medicine for six months by a tribunal in New South Wales after she failed to detect cancer in one patient and operated on the wrong end of the bowel of another. Rate risk | The likelihood of a Reserve Bank interest rate hike has doubled according to market forecasts, as the escalating Middle East crisis once again drives fuel prices higher amid dwindling global oil reserves. Family first | An Aboriginal family preservation program aimed at giving at-risk children space to “tell their own stories” has been launched across New South Wales after a historic investment from the state government. World Houthi warning | Yemen’s Iran-aligned Houthis have warned shipping companies that their tankers risk being attacked if they use Saudi Arabian ports, as the militia moved quickly to enforce a blockade announced the previous day. Nearly 100 US service members have been injured over the last two weeks of escalating strikes on Iran, the Pentagon has said, as Tehran launched a wave of reprisals to the tenth consecutive night of attacks. Follow developments live. Trump subpoena | Donald Trump’s administration sought to subpoena phone records of several New York Times journalists – and even those of some of their relatives – who wrote about the $400m Air Force One jet given to the president by Qatar. ‘No warning’ | Fast-moving wildfires have burned two First Nations communities in Canada to the ground, as members said they were left to self-evacuate without government help or emergency alerts. Cosmic flip | Astronomers have found evidence that the Milky Way was flipped on its side following a direct hit from a wayward galaxy long before the solar system was born. Zendaya ‘hijack’ | The Odyssey star Zendaya has been criticised for wearing 3,000-year-old Iranian jewellery at an event to promote the film with some academics claiming it was a “hijacking” of Iran’s contributions to humanity. Full Story The fallout from Pauline Hanson’s Euro summer Guardian Australia political reporter Josh Butler speaks to Reged Ahmad about how Pauline Hanson’s controversial trip to the UK could play with voters here. In-depth The Victorian government’s signature policy has been the $100bn “Big Build” of road and rail infrastructure projects but it is now threatening to derail Labor under a wave of cost overruns and claims about corruption. Cait Kelly explains. Not the news Gloom has descended on Australia’s musical theatre scene after high-profile cancellations of shows such as Waitress. But a revival of Grey Gardens in Melbourne “glows like a rare gem in a production that fully mines its wit and tragic impulse”, according to Tim Byrne, and gives hope to the sector. Sport World Cup | The head of Spain’s La Liga has accused Fifa of “destroying the football industry” and called for its president, Gianni Infantino, to quit amid suggestions the tournament could be increased to 64 teams in 2030. Football | David Squires finds some laughs in the World Cup’s drab denouement and contrasts it with how the late, great Kevin Keegan embodied the game’s soul. Tennis | The French Open has become the first grand slam tournament to offer players a share of the event’s revenue in a significant step forward in the row over prize money. Media roundup Angus Taylor has quashed suggestions of a Liberal-One Nation merger in South Australia as Malcolm Roberts made new conspiracy-theory claims about Covid deaths, according to the Sydney Morning Herald. Almost 70% of voters think the country is heading in the wrong direction and six in 10 believe immigration levels are too high, a poll in the Australian shows. Up to two-thirds of patients with imminently life-threatening conditions are not being seen within the recommended time at the Royal Hobart hospital, the Mercury reports. What’s happening today Economy | The Reserve Bank of Australia issues the results of its survey of public attitudes. Canberra | Rohan Pie, Nick Coatsworth and Mary Aldred address the National Press Club on illegal tobacco. Courts | Judgment at the federal court in Sydney on the Rebel Wilson defamation battle with Charlotte MacInnes. Sign up Sign up for the Breaking News Australia email If you would like to receive this Morning Mail update to your email inbox every weekday, sign up here, or finish your day with our Afternoon Update newsletter. You can follow the latest in US politics by signing up for This Week in Trumpland. Brain teaser And finally, here are the Guardian’s crosswords to keep you entertained throughout the day. Until tomorrow. Quick crossword Cryptic crossword
chineseTitle: 早间邮报：昆士兰州埋葬 DV 报告，加息担忧加剧，赞达亚的文化“劫持”
chineseSummary: 我们的独家报道揭示了州政府如何搁置一份批评警察的报告；美国与伊朗的冲突威胁澳洲联储加息
chineseContent: 大家早上好。昆士兰州自由党国家党政府一再表示将“将受害者置于该州司法政策的核心”，但今天我们收到了一份独家报告，内容是家庭暴力受害者幸存者小组在猛烈抨击警方处理案件并呼吁进行民事审查后，如何看到他们的报告被埋没。另外：即将上任的工党总统表示，对个人安全的担忧正在阻碍政治家们开展工作，经济学家表示，中东冲突的重新爆发使得加息的可能性更大，赞达亚因佩戴古老的珠宝而被指责缺乏“文化知识”。澳大利亚“有所作为”|即将上任的工党主席凯特·埃利斯表示，政治中的毒性正在蔓延到更广泛的社区和公众辩论中，她警告说，对议员安全的威胁正在限制民选政客的工作方式。独家|昆士兰州政府埋葬了自己的家庭暴力受害者幸存者工作组的调查结果，因为他们的报告严厉批评了警方的应对措施，并呼吁建立“公民审查机制”以升级对警察的投诉。外科医生被禁止|新南威尔士州的一个法庭判定一名外科医生犯有职业不当行为，并禁止其在六个月内行医，原因是她未能检测出一名患者的癌症，并对另一名患者的肠道末端进行了错误的手术。利率风险|根据市场预测，随着中东危机不断升级，在全球石油储备不断减少的情况下，燃油价格再次上涨，储备银行加息的可能性增加了一倍。家庭第一 |新南威尔士州启动了一项原住民家庭保护计划，旨在为高危儿童提供“讲述自己故事”的空间
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
